claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05); Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine whether you're eligible for Child Benefit. To do this properly, I'll need to gather some information about your circumstances and your children.

Let me start with the first question:

**How many children are you inquiring about, and what are their names?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible. 

I have one child - my son Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'd like to gather a few more details about Alex to assess eligibility.

**How old is Alex?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to understand your living situation with Alex.

**Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. That's important information. Let me ask a follow-up question to better understand the situation.

**Who does Alex currently live with?**

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Before I can make a full assessment, I need to check the specific rules about this situation. Let me read the guidance on this.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Based on the guidance I've reviewed, I can see that there's an important consideration here.

When a child lives with someone else (like Alex living with his mum), you can still potentially qualify for Child Benefit, but it depends on whether you're contributing to Alex's upkeep.

**Are you currently making financial contributions towards Alex's upkeep?** This could include things like contributing towards his food, clothes, pocket money, or financial contributions to provide him with somewhere to live. If so, these contributions would need to be at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment for the eligibility to continue beyond 8 weeks.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do help out with some things for Alex. I contribute towards his clothes and bits and pieces when I can.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. I need to understand the extent of your contributions a bit more to properly assess your eligibility.

**Are your contributions towards Alex's clothes and other items roughly equivalent to, or more than, the current Child Benefit payment amount?** 

In other words, would you say you're contributing around £25 per week or more on average (this is approximately the current Child Benefit rate for a first child)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, not quite that much. I contribute about £10 a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the guidance from the UK government, here's my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Summary

**You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

- Alex lives with his mother, not with you
- According to the rules, when a child lives with someone else, you can only continue receiving Child Benefit beyond the initial 8 weeks if your financial contributions to the child's upkeep are **equal to or greater than** the Child Benefit payment amount
- You're currently contributing approximately £10 per week, but the required amount is around £25 per week
- Since your contributions fall short of this threshold, you don't meet the eligibility criteria for ongoing Child Benefit

**If you were to increase your contributions to at least £25 per week or more**, you could potentially become eligible. However, at your current contribution level, you would not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about this decision?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and lives with his mother, not with you. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, when a child lives with someone else, you can normally get Child Benefit for 8 weeks after the child goes to live with them. However, you can continue to receive payments for longer if you contribute to the child's upkeep by an amount equal to or greater than the Child Benefit payment itself. You have stated that you contribute approximately £10 per week towards Alex's clothes and other items. The current Child Benefit rate for a first child is approximately £25 per week. Since your contributions of £10 per week are less than the required amount, you do not meet the criteria to continue receiving Child Benefit beyond the initial 8-week period. Therefore, you are not eligible for ongoing Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. While you do make some financial contributions towards his upkeep, these contributions (approximately £10 per week) fall short of the required amount (approximately £25 per week, matching the Child Benefit payment rate). For eligibility to continue beyond the initial 8 weeks when a child lives with someone else, your contributions must be equal to or greater than the Child Benefit payment amount. Unless you increase your contributions to meet this threshold, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex."

According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, when a child lives with someone else, you can normally get Child Benefit for 8 weeks after the child goes to live with them. However, you can continue to receive payments for longer if you contribute to the child's upkeep by an amount equal to or greater than the Child Benefit payment itself. You have stated that you contribute approximately £10 per week towards Alex's clothes and other items. The current Child Benefit rate for a first child is approximately £25 per week. Since your contributions of £10 per week are less than the required amount, you do not meet the criteria to continue receiving Child Benefit beyond the initial 8-week period. Therefore, you are not eligible for ongoing Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: You are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. While you do make some financial contributions towards his upkeep, these contributions (approximately £10 per week) fall short of the required amount (approximately £25 per week, matching the Child Benefit payment rate). For eligibility to continue beyond the initial 8 weeks when a child lives with someone else, your contributions must be equal to or greater than the Child Benefit payment amount. Unless you increase your contributions to meet this threshold, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex."
